critic_raw: The primary methodological concern is that reporting precision, harmful-error rate, and coverage only for tickets above a 0.92 confidence threshold can give a misleadingly favorable picture of the classifier's performance. When you exclude all tickets the model declines to answer, you are not measuring the model's ability to handle the full incoming ticket distribution — you are measuring its performance on the subset it selects itself. A model that declines 99% of inputs and answers only the easiest 1% could look flawless on the reported metric while being nearly useless as an automation tool. The failure to report any statistics on the 63% of tickets routed to humans — including whether the model's abstentions are systematically biased toward certain ticket types, customer segments, or topic areas — makes it impossible to assess whether the automation is fair and complete in its coverage claims. There is also a concern about whether the 0.92 threshold was genuinely set prospectively or was selected based on development-set exploration to hit the 1% harmful-error target.
defender_raw: The evaluation design is appropriate for what is actually being claimed and deployed. The product is a selective-prediction system that intentionally abstains on uncertain tickets and routes them to human agents. Evaluating such a system requires measuring (1) performance on the tickets the system accepts, and (2) coverage — the share of total volume that gets automated. Both are reported. The report provides harmful-error rate of 0.7% among auto-resolved tickets (below the pre-specified 1% ceiling) and coverage of 37% (above the pre-specified 30% floor). The coverage number directly answers the concern about abstentions: the report is transparent that 63% of tickets go to humans. The threshold was written into a pre-registration before model development began — not selected after testing. This is a fundamentally different situation from cherry-picking. The claim is explicitly about the selective-prediction pipeline, not about the model's universal classification performance. A different organization wanting different automation rates would appropriately use a different threshold.
adjudication_raw: The critic raises a valid concern about selective reporting in the abstract, but the defender demonstrates that the specific concern does not apply here. The pre-registered threshold directly addresses the worry about post-hoc selection. The coverage metric answers the concern about what happens to abstained tickets. The claim is appropriately scoped to the selective-prediction system, not to a universal classifier. The critic's point about potential bias in abstentions is worth flagging as a limitation but does not invalidate the reported metrics. The defender's argument prevails because the evaluation protocol matches the deployed system's design and claims.
all_issues_raised: selective reporting concern — metrics only above threshold
potential abstention bias toward certain ticket types
uncertainty about whether threshold was genuinely pre-registered
